Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every production failure has a signature. Most engineers learn to read them the hard way.

11 min read
Just now

--

There is a specific frustration that every backend engineer experiences at some point in their career.
You are looking in the right place. The failing service. The affected endpoint. The relevant code path. Everything your instincts point at is technically correct. The failure is in the component you’re investigating. The logs confirm it. The metrics confirm it. The error messages confirm it.
And you still can’t find the cause.
You look harder. You go deeper into the code. You add more logging. You read more carefully. You spend another thirty minutes in a place that should contain the answer.
The answer is not there.
It is one layer away from where you’re looking. In the infrastructure the service runs on. In the connection management between the service and its dependencies. In the configuration that the service reads at startup. In the behavior of the framework that the service is built on.
The code is correct. The layer beneath the code is not.
This is the most common form of production failure that takes hours when it should take minutes. Not because the cause is complex. Because the investigation is at the right level of abstraction for the symptoms and the wrong level of abstraction for the cause.

How Layers Work in Production Failures
Every production system is a stack of layers.
The application code is the layer most engineers are comfortable with. It is what they write, what they review, what they test. It is where the investigation naturally goes when something fails.
Below the application code is the framework layer. Spring Boot, Django, Express, Rails. The framework handles request routing, dependency injection, transaction management, connection pooling, serialization. Most engineers interact with the framework through configuration and annotations rather than by reading its source code.
Below the framework layer is the runtime layer. The JVM, the Python interpreter, the Node.js event loop. The runtime manages memory, garbage collection, thread scheduling, I/O operations.
Below the runtime layer is the infrastructure layer. The operating system, the container, the orchestration platform. File descriptors, network interfaces, disk I/O, process scheduling.
Below the infrastructure layer is the dependency layer. The database, the cache, the message queue, the external APIs. The services that the application depends on to function.
A production failure can originate at any layer. The symptoms usually appear at the application layer, where the user-facing behavior is affected. The cause is often two or three layers removed from where the symptoms appear.
The investigation that goes to the application layer and stays there will find symptoms but not causes when the cause is in a different layer.
The investigation that asks which layer the cause is most likely in before committing to a direction will find the cause significantly faster.

The Framework Layer Failures Nobody Expects
The framework layer is the most consistently underestimated source of production failures in modern backend systems.
Frameworks are supposed to handle complexity. They exist to let engineers write application code without thinking about the underlying plumbing. They are successful at this most of the time.
The times they are not successful are the times when the framework’s behavior under specific conditions diverges from what the engineer assumes based on normal conditions.
These divergences are not bugs in the framework. They are documented behaviors that produce unexpected outcomes when combined with specific application patterns.
The @Transactional annotation in Spring is the example I return to most often.
The mental model most Spring engineers have: annotate a method with @Transactional and Spring wraps it in a database transaction. If the method throws an exception, the transaction rolls back. The data is safe.
This mental model is correct in one specific scenario. When the @Transactional method is called from outside the bean, through the Spring proxy that intercepts the call and applies transaction management.
When the @Transactional method is called from inside the same bean, the call goes directly to the method without passing through the proxy. The proxy is not involved. The transaction management is not applied. The annotation does nothing.
This behavior is documented. It is also consistently surprising to engineers who encounter it in production for the first time, because the code looks exactly the same whether the proxy is involved or not.
The application layer investigation finds nothing wrong. The application code is correct. The framework layer investigation finds the missing proxy, which is invisible at the application layer.
The @Async annotation has the same structure. Annotate a method with @Async and Spring executes it in a separate thread pool, freeing the calling thread to return immediately. Except when the @Async method is called internally from within the same bean, in which case the proxy is bypassed and the method executes synchronously in the calling thread. The annotation does nothing.
The application layer investigation finds nothing wrong with the async code. The framework layer investigation finds that the method is executing synchronously because the proxy is not involved.
Both of these failures produce production behavior that is impossible to reproduce in unit tests, difficult to reproduce in integration tests without specific test configuration, and consistently surprising because the annotation says one thing and the runtime behavior is another.
The investigation that understands this goes to the framework layer immediately when seeing behavior that contradicts what the annotations should produce. The investigation that stays at the application layer searches the application code for a problem that is not there.

The Runtime Layer Failures That Look Like Application Bugs
The runtime layer produces a category of production failures that are uniquely frustrating to diagnose because the symptoms look exactly like application bugs.
Memory pressure is the most common.
A Java application under heavy load starts responding slowly. Response times increase gradually. The garbage collector is running frequently. The application is not doing anything different from what it always does. The load is within the expected range.
The application layer investigation looks at what the application is doing. The application code is the same code that handles the load correctly under normal conditions. Nothing has changed.
The runtime layer investigation looks at what the JVM is doing. The heap is under pressure. Garbage collection is consuming an increasing percentage of CPU time. Objects are surviving into the old generation at a rate the GC cannot keep up with.
The application layer symptom is slow responses. The runtime layer cause is GC pressure. The fix is not in the application code. It is in the heap configuration, the GC algorithm selection, or the memory allocation patterns of the application.
An engineer investigating at the application layer will not find this. An engineer who asks “which layer is this cause in” will recognize the GC pressure signature and shift to the runtime layer.
The JVM also produces thread-related failures that look like application bugs.
A deadlock between two threads produces an application that appears to hang on specific operations. The application layer investigation sees requests that never complete. It looks like an infinite loop or a blocking operation in the application code. The runtime layer investigation looks at the thread dump and finds two threads waiting for locks held by each other.
The application code may have no visible bug. The deadlock emerges from the interaction of two code paths that each look correct in isolation and produce a deadlock when they execute concurrently under specific timing conditions.
Thread dumps are the diagnostic tool for this class of failure. They are available at the runtime layer. They are invisible at the application layer.

The Infrastructure Layer Failures That Look Like Service Failures
The infrastructure layer produces the failures that are most often misattributed to the services running on top of it.
File descriptor exhaustion is a reliable example.
A Linux process can have a maximum number of open files at any given time. Open files include network sockets, which include every open connection the service has to clients, databases, message queues, and other services. When the file descriptor limit is reached, the process cannot open new connections. It cannot accept new requests. It looks like the service is down.
The application layer investigation sees a service that cannot accept connections. The error messages mention connection failures. The investigation goes to the application code and the network configuration of the application.
The infrastructure layer investigation checks the file descriptor limit and the current file descriptor usage. If the process is at its limit, that is the cause. The application is not broken. The infrastructure constraint is broken.
The fix is increasing the file descriptor limit, which is an operating system configuration change, not an application change. The application layer investigation will not find this fix.
OOM kills are similar. The Linux out-of-memory killer terminates processes when system memory is exhausted. The process is not crashed. It is killed by the operating system because the system needed the memory.
The application layer investigation sees a service that restarted unexpectedly. The restart looks like a crash. The investigation looks for application crashes, unhandled exceptions, and bugs that might cause the process to terminate.
The infrastructure layer investigation checks the OOM killer logs. If the process was OOM killed, that is in the kernel logs, not in the application logs. The application did not crash. The operating system killed it.
The fix involves memory configuration, heap limits, and container resource constraints. The application layer investigation will not find it because the process did not crash. It was killed.
Container environments add a layer of infrastructure above the operating system and produce their own category of failures.
A container that is consistently restarted looks like an application that is consistently crashing. The investigation goes to the application logs. The application logs may show nothing wrong because the container is being restarted by the orchestration platform before the application reaches the point where it would log an error.
The infrastructure layer investigation looks at the container runtime logs and the orchestration platform events. Kubernetes events show why a pod was terminated. Liveness probe failures, OOM kills, and resource limit violations all appear in the events, not in the application logs.

The Dependency Layer Failures That Look Like Local Failures
The dependency layer is the most commonly misidentified source of production failures.
When an application that depends on a database starts returning errors, the natural investigation goes to the application’s database interaction code. The queries. The connection handling. The ORM configuration.
When an application that depends on an external API starts failing, the investigation goes to the API client code. The request construction. The response parsing. The error handling.
These investigations are correct in that the failure is in the interaction with the dependency. They are incorrect if they stop at the application layer rather than examining the dependency layer.
The dependency layer investigation asks: what is the dependency actually doing?
For a database: is the database healthy? Are queries executing normally? Is there lock contention?
